text: The surgeon begins lateral dissection by detaching the natural adhesions of the sigmoid colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure. After completing the lateral dissection, the Toldt's fascia is separated from the mesocolon, revealing the left gonadal vessel and left ureter beneath.

The surgeon exposes the mesosigmoid by grasping the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, initiating medial dissection from the sacral promontory toward the IMA root. A peritoneal window is created between the lateral and medial dissection planes, followed by apical node dissection starting at the anticipated IMA bifurcation, where the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) typically traverses.

The surgeon carefully performs IMA node dissection, avoiding injury to the superior hypogastric nerve plexus and preaortic nerve plexus. Following the completion of the node dissection, the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is ligated, and the supravector artery is ligated at the IMA bifurcation, with preservation of the left colic artery.

The surgeon begins pelvic dissection at the sacral promontory, where abundant lusoreolar tissues are visible. The rectal proper fascia is carefully dissected away from the pre-hypogastric nerve fascia, which covers both hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon uses a tape to hang the mobilized rectum, providing traction. A U-shaped incision is made at the peritoneal reflection, exposing the seminal vesicles and nonvertebral fascia. During the posterior lateral pelvic dissection, the surgeon carefully avoids injuring the hypogastric nerves and inferior hypogastric nerve plexus, which are visible running downward and tethering the rectal proper fascia.

The surgeon incises the rectal sacral fascia, revealing the pelvic floor. Anterolateral dissection is performed with caution to avoid injuring the inferior hypogastric nerve plexus, which is visible continuing towards the neurovascular bundles leading to the genitourinary organs.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum and divides the mesorectum at the distal margin. The rectum is resected using linear staplers, and a mini-laparotomy site is prepared for specimen extraction.
